does: This paper conducts a systematic evaluation of frontier large language models (GPT-5-Thinking, Gemini-3-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-4.5-Thinking, and Grok-4) on PhD-level mathematical reasoning by tasking them with generating formal LaTeX proofs for lemmas and exercises from the canonical textbook Randomized Algorithms by Motwani and Raghavan. The work establishes a rigorous benchmark grounded in established graduate-level theoretical computer science curriculum, measuring how well current frontier models handle formal mathematical proof generation across the breadth of randomized algorithms theory. The benchmark provides concrete performance metrics on this challenging domain, revealing baseline capabilities and limitations of these models on canonical, peer-reviewed mathematical material that represents the kind of rigorous reasoning expected in advanced computer science.
connects: This work responds directly to recent claims about LLM capabilities in mathematical discovery and scientific reasoning, building on demonstrations that frontier models can propose novel constructions, improve existing bounds, and contribute meaningfully to scientific workflows. It extends the emerging line of evaluation work assessing LLMs on formal mathematics, complementing efforts to benchmark models on theorem proving, competition mathematics, and research-level problems. The paper situates itself in the context of growing interest in measuring whether advanced LLMs can reason rigorously about graduate-level material, bridging gaps between anecdotal reports of breakthroughs and systematic assessment. By targeting a specific canonical textbook rather than ad hoc problem collections, it addresses the need for standardized evaluation on established mathematical curricula that have proven their worth in training mathematicians and computer scientists.
enables: This benchmark establishes a foundation for systematic comparison of frontier LLM capabilities on formal mathematical reasoning, enabling researchers to track progress as new models emerge and extended reasoning approaches improve. The work points toward developing better evaluation frameworks for assessing LLM contributions to mathematical research, potentially identifying specific algorithmic concepts or proof strategies where models struggle. It opens avenues for analyzing failure modes in formal proof generation and investigating how extended reasoning mechanisms handle complex mathematical arguments. The benchmark also enables future work on improving LLM mathematical reasoning through targeted fine-tuning or training on formal mathematics, and could inform strategies for augmenting LLMs with symbolic reasoning tools or formal verification to increase reliability on graduate-level mathematical tasks.
